Category: --- UNIVERSAL LIFECYCLE STATUSES: the basic states any construction record can be in ---

Description: Record is active and available for action — the entry point for most construction records. Indicates the item has been created and is part of the active workflow. Every record type has some version of 'open' as its initial actionable state.
Common Values: Open, Active, New
Source Systems: Procore; P6; Sage 300; Viewpoint Vista; CMiC; PlanGrid; Bluebeam
Procore Coverage: High — status fields exist on virtually every Procore table, universal adoption
Benchmarking Use: Compare record creation-to-close ratios across projects to identify workflow efficiency
Notes: Some systems use 'Active' or 'New' instead of 'Open'. UCDM should normalize to 'Open' as the universal starting state.

Description: Work has begun on the record but is not yet complete. Indicates active effort by a responsible party — distinguishes items being worked on from those sitting idle. Common on tasks, action plans, inspections, and punch items.
Common Values: In Progress, Active, Working
Source Systems: Procore; P6; MS Project; Sage 300; PlanGrid
Procore Coverage: High — structured status value on action plans, punch items, and task-based records
Benchmarking Use: Track percentage of records in active work vs. idle to measure team throughput
Notes: Not all record types use 'In Progress' — RFIs and submittals typically go Submitted → Under Review. Map carefully per record type.

Description: Record exists but has not been formally issued into the workflow. Editable, not yet visible to reviewers or counterparties. The pre-submission holding state where the creator refines before committing.
Common Values: Draft, Preliminary, Not Issued
Procore Coverage: High — Draft status on change events, RFIs, submittals, correspondences, commitments
Benchmarking Use: Compare draft-to-submission conversion rates — high draft abandonment signals unclear processes
Notes: Draft items should be excluded from cycle time calculations. Only count from submission forward.

Description: Record has completed its lifecycle — no further action required. Terminal state indicating the workflow has run its course, whether resolved successfully or not. Distinct from Void (cancelled) and Approved (decision).
Common Values: Closed, Complete, Resolved, Done
Source Systems: Procore; P6; Sage 300; Viewpoint; CMiC; MS Project
Procore Coverage: High — terminal status on most record types
Benchmarking Use: Closure rates and average time-to-close are primary workflow efficiency benchmarks
Notes: Some systems use 'Complete' or 'Resolved'. Normalize to 'Closed' for lifecycle analysis. Check whether closed items had a resolution or were just abandoned.

Description: Record has been invalidated or cancelled — the work was never completed, just abandoned. Distinct from Closed (completed lifecycle). Voided records should be excluded from most metrics but tracked as a signal of rework or process waste.
Common Values: Void, Cancelled, Withdrawn, Deleted
Procore Coverage: High — Void status on change events, commitments, change orders
Benchmarking Use: Void rates by record type reveal process waste — high void rate on COs signals poor scoping
Notes: Voided CEs use mapped_to_status != 'void' filter. Voided CCOs use status = 'Void'. Always exclude from cost aggregations.

Description: Record is paused, awaiting external input, owner decision, or other blocking condition. Not cancelled — expected to resume. Common when waiting for design clarification, material delivery, or client direction.
Common Values: On Hold, Suspended, Waiting, Deferred
Procore Coverage: Medium — available as status on some records but not a dedicated workflow state
Benchmarking Use: Hold duration and hold frequency reveal external dependency patterns and decision bottlenecks
Notes: On Hold is often used inconsistently — some teams use it for 'I don't know what to do with this'. Consider tracking hold reason for meaningful analysis.

Description: Record has passed its due date without reaching its expected next state. A computed status based on comparing due_date to current_date and record status. Indicates workflow stall requiring attention.
Common Values: Overdue, Past Due, Late
Source Systems: Procore; P6; MS Project; PlanGrid; Fieldwire
Procore Coverage: High — overdue flag on submittals, due_date_variance on RFIs, computable on any record with due date
Benchmarking Use: Overdue rates by record type and responsible party are core accountability metrics
Notes: Procore has a native 'overdue' boolean on submittals and due_date_variance on RFIs. For other records, overdue must be computed from due_date + status.

Category: --- APPROVAL & REVIEW STATUSES: states specific to formal review and approval cycles ---

Description: Awaiting action from the next responsible party. The record has entered the workflow but the reviewer has not yet begun evaluation. Pre-action holding state that starts the response time clock.
Common Values: Pending, Awaiting Review, Queued
Source Systems: Procore; Bluebeam; Newforma; PlanGrid; Submittal Exchange
Procore Coverage: High — Pending status on commitments, change orders, action plans
Benchmarking Use: Pending queue depth per reviewer reveals capacity constraints and bottleneck individuals
Notes: Pending vs. Submitted distinction matters: Pending = in queue but not picked up. Submitted = formally sent. Some systems conflate these.

Description: Formally sent to the reviewer or approver — the official handoff point. Clock starts on contractual response time. The creator has completed their work and is waiting for a decision.
Common Values: Submitted, Issued, Sent for Review
Source Systems: Procore; Bluebeam; Newforma; Submittal Exchange; PlanGrid
Procore Coverage: High — Submitted status on submittals, RFIs, change events, correspondences
Benchmarking Use: Submission volume over time reveals project phase intensity — early submittals peak in design, late ones signal problems
Notes: Track submitted_date as the official workflow start for cycle time calculations.

Description: Actively being evaluated by the responsible party. Between submitted and decision. Some systems track this explicitly, others infer it from ball-in-court assignment.
Common Values: Under Review, In Review, Being Reviewed
Procore Coverage: Medium — some records have explicit Under Review status, others rely on ball-in-court to infer review activity
Benchmarking Use: Time spent in review vs. pending reveals whether delays are caused by queue depth or actual review complexity
Notes: Not universally tracked as a distinct status. Many systems jump from Submitted → Approved/Rejected without explicit review state.

Description: Accepted without conditions — authorizes the associated work, cost, or material to proceed. The positive terminal decision in an approval workflow. On financial records, triggers commitment of funds.
Common Values: Approved, Accepted, Authorized
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint; CMiC; Bluebeam; Newforma
Procore Coverage: High — Approved status on submittals, commitments, change orders, budget changes (96.7% of CCOs are Approved)
Benchmarking Use: Approval rates and time-to-approval are fundamental workflow health metrics
Notes: CCOs are 96.7% approved in Procore — rejection is rare once a CCO is created. CEs have more status variety.

Description: Accepted with conditions, corrections, or minor modifications. Common on submittals — fabrication may proceed but noted changes must be incorporated. The 'yes, but' decision that avoids full rejection.
Common Values: Approved as Noted, Approved with Comments, Conditional Approval
Procore Coverage: High — native submittal status in Procore, structured value
Benchmarking Use: Track 'as noted' frequency by spec section to identify which trades produce the most revision-prone submittals
Notes: Approved as Noted should be counted as approved for throughput metrics but flagged for quality metrics — it signals rework potential.

Description: Not accepted — requires rework before resubmission. A hard stop that sends the record back to the originator. Resets the workflow cycle. Should always include rejection reason for tracking.
Common Values: Rejected, Not Approved, Returned, Denied
Procore Coverage: High — native status on submittals, RFIs, and approval workflows
Benchmarking Use: Rejection rates by reviewer, trade, and spec section identify quality gaps and problem areas
Notes: Rejection reason is critical for root cause analysis but often unstructured free text. Cross-reference with spec section for pattern detection.

Description: Similar to rejected but with specific revision guidance — the reviewer has identified what needs to change. Softer than rejection, implies the approach is correct but execution needs refinement. Common on submittals.
Common Values: Revise and Resubmit, Revise & Resubmit, Resubmit
Procore Coverage: High — native submittal status in Procore
Benchmarking Use: Resubmission rates and revision cycles per submittal reveal design coordination quality
Notes: Track revision count per submittal. More than 2 cycles signals a breakdown in pre-submission coordination.

Category: --- FIELD DISPOSITION STATUSES: states from field inspection and quality observation workflows ---

Description: Field work is complete and awaiting quality inspection, superintendent sign-off, or third-party verification. The handoff point between field crews and quality/management teams.
Common Values: Ready for Review, Ready for Inspection, Complete — Pending QC
Procore Coverage: Medium — workflow_status value on punch items and observations, but not a universal explicit state
Benchmarking Use: Tracks field-to-QC handoff velocity — long Ready for Review times indicate inspection bottleneck
Notes: In Procore, this maps to specific workflow_status values on punch items. Not all record types have this state.

Description: Inspection item meets the specified requirements — pass disposition. The field evaluator has verified the work complies with plans, specs, or standards. Moves the item toward closure.
Common Values: Conforming, Pass, Satisfactory, Meets Requirements
Procore Coverage: High — inspection_items.status = 'Conforming' is a structured native value
Benchmarking Use: Conformance rates by inspection type, trade, and project phase reveal quality performance patterns
Notes: In Procore, inspection_items.status values are capitalized: 'Conforming' (not 'conforming'). Deficiency rate across all inspections is typically 2-6%.

Description: Inspection item fails to meet requirements — requires corrective action. A formal finding that the installed work does not comply with plans, specifications, or applicable codes. Triggers rework cycle.
Common Values: Deficient, Fail, Non-Conforming, Does Not Meet Requirements
Procore Coverage: High — inspection_items.status = 'Deficient' is a structured native value
Benchmarking Use: Deficiency rates per trade, spec section, and project type are core quality benchmarks
Notes: In Procore, 'Deficient' (capitalized). Track deficiency-to-resolution cycle time. Also see observation.type_name for severity classification.

Description: Record or inspection item does not apply in this context — excluded from evaluation. Used when an inspection checklist item is irrelevant to the specific work being inspected.
Common Values: Not Applicable, N/A, Excluded, Not Inspected
Procore Coverage: High — inspection_items.status = 'Not Applicable' is a structured native value
Benchmarking Use: High N/A rates on inspection templates signal poorly configured checklists — template optimization opportunity
Notes: 'Not Applicable' and 'Uninspected' are distinct in Procore. N/A = doesn't apply. Uninspected = applies but wasn't checked. 'Neutral' also exists as a non-disposition.

Category: --- WORKFLOW COMPONENTS: the operational building blocks of construction workflows ---

Description: An individual stage in a multi-step approval or review process. Defines who must act, what action is required, and the sequence position. The atomic unit of workflow configuration.
Common Values: Step 1: PM Review → Step 2: Architect Review → Step 3: Owner Approval
Source Systems: Procore; Newforma; Aconex; Oracle Aconex; Kahua
Procore Coverage: Medium — workflow_steps tables exist for several record types but most are not import-ready in the gold layer
Benchmarking Use: Step count per workflow type reveals process complexity — simpler workflows correlate with faster throughput
Notes: Procore has *_workflow_steps tables for budget changes, commitments, COs, owner/sub invoices. Most are NOT ready (N) in gold layer except owner_invoice.

Description: Ordered sequence of reviewers and approvers that a record must pass through before reaching a terminal state. Defines the review hierarchy, required vs. optional reviewers, and fallback rules.
Common Values: Linear chain, parallel reviewers, hybrid (parallel then sequential)
Source Systems: Procore; Newforma; Aconex; Kahua; Submittal Exchange
Procore Coverage: Medium — submittal_approvers table (Y) tracks approval chains, but configuration of chain rules is limited in gold layer
Benchmarking Use: Average chain length by record type benchmarks process overhead — longer chains increase cycle time
Notes: In Procore, submittal_approvers defines the approval chain with sequence and response tracking. Most other record types have simpler single-approver patterns.

Description: The party currently responsible for taking the next action on a record. Construction-specific accountability term — at any moment, someone 'has the ball'. Changes as the record moves through workflow steps.
Common Values: Architect, GC PM, Subcontractor, Owner, Engineer
Procore Coverage: High — dedicated rfis_bic and submittal_ball_in_courts tables, plus ball_in_court fields on records
Benchmarking Use: Ball-in-court aging by party type reveals who causes the most workflow delays — actionable accountability metric
Notes: In Procore, BIC is tracked via dedicated tables (rfis_bic, submittal_ball_in_courts) and inline fields. DocuSign overlay adds docusign_ball_in_court_status.

Description: Set of parties notified when a record is created, changes status, or receives a response. Ensures relevant stakeholders stay informed without being in the approval chain. Notification, not action.
Common Values: PM, Superintendent, Owner Rep, Architect, Sub Foreman
Procore Coverage: High — rfi_distributions, submittal_distributions, company/project_distribution_groups tables all ready (Y)
Benchmarking Use: Distribution list breadth correlates with project communication health — narrow lists miss stakeholders
Notes: Distribution groups can be configured at company level (reusable) or project level (custom). Both table types exist in Procore.

Description: A reviewer's formal action at a workflow step — approve, reject, forward, return, or comment. The decision record that advances (or returns) the workflow. Timestamped for cycle time calculation.
Common Values: Approve, Reject, Forward, Return, Comment, No Response Required
Procore Coverage: Medium — owner_invoice_workflow_responses (Y), but most *_workflow_responses tables are not import-ready
Benchmarking Use: Response patterns by reviewer reveal decision-making behavior — some approvers rubber-stamp, others consistently add conditions
Notes: Procore has *_workflow_responses tables for budget changes, commitments, COs, invoices. Most NOT ready in gold layer except owner_invoice.

Description: The date by which action must be completed on a record or workflow step. Contractually significant — missed due dates can trigger liquidated damages, delay claims, or escalation.
Common Values: 7-day RFI response, 14-day submittal review, 30-day CO approval
Source Systems: Procore; P6; MS Project; Newforma; PlanGrid
Procore Coverage: High — due_date fields on RFIs, submittals, action plans, meeting items, punch items
Benchmarking Use: Due date compliance rates are fundamental project management health indicators
Notes: RFI due_date_variance and submittal overdue flag are native Procore fields. For other records, compare due_date to action_date.

Description: The elapsed time between submission and response — either the contractual allowance or the actual measured duration. Critical for project pacing — slow responses cascade into schedule delays.
Common Values: 7 days (RFI), 14 days (submittal), 21 days (complex submittal), 30 days (CO)
Procore Coverage: Medium — computable from date fields but not a native stored metric. due_date_variance on RFIs is the closest
Benchmarking Use: Average response time by record type, reviewer, and project phase benchmarks workflow performance
Notes: Must be computed: response_date - submitted_date. Filter out weekends/holidays for business days. Cross-reference with contract-specified allowances.

Description: Elevation of a stalled workflow item to a higher authority when the responsible party fails to act within the allowed response time. The safety valve for stuck workflows.
Common Values: Auto-escalate after 7 days, notify PM after 3 days, alert exec after 14 days
Procore Coverage: None — no escalation tracking or automation in Procore. Manual process via email/phone.
Benchmarking Use: Escalation frequency reveals systemic bottlenecks — high escalation rates signal broken workflows or overwhelmed reviewers
Notes: Procore does not have native escalation. This is a gap — most enterprise PM systems include configurable escalation rules.

Description: Temporary or permanent transfer of approval authority from one party to another. Required when the primary reviewer is unavailable — prevents workflow stalls during vacations, role changes, or emergencies.
Common Values: Out-of-office delegation, role-based delegation, proxy approval
Procore Coverage: None — no delegation capability in Procore. Workaround: manually reassign ball-in-court.
Benchmarking Use: Delegation frequency and patterns reveal organizational resilience — high delegation need with no capability signals single-point-of-failure risk
Notes: Procore lacks formal delegation. Teams work around this by manually changing BIC or having admins act on behalf of others.

Category: --- WORKFLOW PATTERNS: standard workflow types used across construction record types ---

Description: Approvers review in a defined order — each must act before the next receives the item. Ensures layered review (PM → Architect → Owner). Most formal and slowest pattern.
Common Values: Budget change: PM → Controller → Exec. CO: PM → Owner.
Procore Coverage: Medium — workflow_steps tables define sequence for some records (owner_invoice ready), but most workflow tables not in gold layer
Benchmarking Use: Compare sequential chain length to cycle time — each additional step adds measurable days
Notes: In Procore, sequential approval is configured per record type. The number of steps varies by company configuration.

Description: Multiple reviewers evaluate simultaneously — all must respond before the workflow advances. Faster than sequential but requires all parties to be available. Common on design submittals with multiple discipline reviewers.
Common Values: Structural + MEP + Architectural review on shop drawings
Procore Coverage: Medium — submittal_approvers supports parallel reviewer assignment, but parallel vs. sequential distinction isn't always explicit
Benchmarking Use: Parallel review effectiveness depends on reviewer response time variance — one slow reviewer negates the parallel benefit
Notes: submittal_approvers tracks individual reviewer responses. Parallel patterns can be inferred when multiple approvers have the same sequence position.

Description: Single submission followed by one or more approval decisions. The simplest and most common construction workflow pattern — the creator sends, the reviewer decides.
Common Values: Submittal → Approved. CO → Approved. Invoice → Paid.
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint; CMiC; Bluebeam; PlanGrid
Procore Coverage: High — basic status field pattern on virtually every record type in Procore
Benchmarking Use: Submit-and-approve cycle time is the most universally comparable workflow metric
Notes: This is the default pattern. More complex patterns (sequential, parallel) are variations on this base.

Description: A question or information request requiring a formal written response. The RFI pattern — question asked, answer provided, response becomes part of the project record. Response may have cost/schedule impact.
Common Values: RFI: Question → Response → Closed. Correspondence: Sent → Replied → Archived.
Source Systems: Procore; Newforma; PlanGrid; Aconex; Oracle Unifier
Procore Coverage: High — RFI workflow fully structured with question, response, BIC, predicted_topic, cost/schedule impact tracking
Benchmarking Use: Review-and-respond metrics (volume, cycle time, cost impact rate) are among the strongest project health indicators
Notes: RFIs are the most structured Review & Respond implementation. Correspondences follow a similar pattern with less structure.

Description: Field evaluation of installed work against defined criteria, resulting in a conforming/deficient judgment. The quality assurance pattern — inspector walks the work, checks each item, renders a disposition.
Common Values: Inspection: Uninspected → Conforming/Deficient. Observation: Open → Closed.
Procore Coverage: High — inspection_items with structured status (Conforming/Deficient/Not Applicable), observations with workflow_status
Benchmarking Use: Inspect-and-disposition pass rates by trade, phase, and template type are core quality benchmarks
Notes: The inspect-and-disposition pattern generates the richest quality signal when combined with keyword analysis on item names.

Description: Simplest lifecycle: an item is created, worked on, and closed. No formal approval chain — the responsible party resolves and closes. The punch list and field finding pattern.
Common Values: Punch: Open → In Progress → Closed. Observation: Open → Ready for Review → Closed.
Procore Coverage: High — punch items and observations have structured workflow_status with clear create-to-close lifecycle
Benchmarking Use: Create-to-close cycle time by priority, trade, and category reveals field responsiveness
Notes: Simple but high-volume. Punch items (~22.3M records) and observations (~1.3M) generate massive workflow data for benchmarking.

Description: A change progresses through independent budget, cost, and revenue stages — each with its own approval status. The change management pattern where a single event triggers parallel financial workflows across different ledgers.
Common Values: Budget stage: Open → Approved → Closed. Cost stage: Pending → Approved. Revenue stage: Active → Complete.
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint; CMiC; Oracle Unifier
Procore Coverage: High — budget_stage_status, cost_stage_status, revenue_stage_status columns on change event line items with structured values
Benchmarking Use: Cross-stage velocity (how fast changes move from budget recognition to cost commitment to revenue billing) reveals financial process maturity
Notes: Three independent stage statuses on each CE line item. Some stages may be skipped (e.g., no revenue stage if not billing T&M).

Category: --- FINANCIAL WORKFLOW STAGES: multi-stage lifecycle tracking for change management ---

Description: The budget approval phase of a change event — tracks whether the budget impact has been formally recognized and approved. First stage in the three-stage financial workflow. Controls whether the project budget is adjusted.
Common Values: Open, Approved, Closed
Procore Coverage: High — budget_stage_status is a structured column on change event line items
Benchmarking Use: Budget stage approval velocity reveals how quickly orgs recognize cost changes — slow recognition creates hidden overruns
Notes: budget_stage_in_status_since tracks dwell time. Budget stage can be approved while cost/revenue stages remain pending.

Description: The cost commitment phase of a change — tracks whether costs have been formally committed to subcontractors or vendors via change orders. Second stage. Controls whether the commitment is updated.
Common Values: Pending, Approved, Rejected
Procore Coverage: High — cost_stage_status is a structured column on change event line items
Benchmarking Use: Cost stage lag (time from budget approval to cost commitment) reveals procurement bottleneck duration
Notes: cost_stage_in_status_since tracks dwell time. The CE→CPCO→CCO chain is the Procore path from cost stage to executed change order.

Description: The owner billing phase of a change — tracks whether the approved change has been incorporated into owner invoicing. Third and final stage. Controls whether the GC bills the owner for the change.
Common Values: Active, Complete
Procore Coverage: High — revenue_stage_status is a structured column on change event line items
Benchmarking Use: Revenue stage completion rate reveals billing capture — incomplete revenue stages mean approved changes not yet billed
Notes: revenue_stage_in_status_since tracks dwell time. Not all changes have a revenue component (e.g., backcharges deducted, not billed).

Description: The end-to-end progression of a change from identification through budget recognition, cost commitment, and revenue billing. The composite view across all three financial stages for a single change event.
Industry Standard: AACE 10S-90; PMI PMBOK Integrated Change Control
Common Values: Identified → Budget Approved → Cost Committed → Revenue Billed → Closed
Source Systems: Procore; Sage 300; Viewpoint; CMiC; Oracle Unifier
Procore Coverage: High — change_event_status plus three stage statuses provide full lifecycle visibility. mapped_to_status normalizes.
Benchmarking Use: Full lifecycle velocity (identification to close) is the ultimate change management efficiency metric
Notes: mapped_to_status normalizes domain-specific statuses for cross-record comparison. Use mapped_to_status != 'void' to exclude voided changes.

Description: A standardized status field that normalizes domain-specific statuses into a common vocabulary for cross-record comparison. Allows consistent filtering and aggregation across record types that use different status taxonomies.
Common Values: Open, Pending, Approved, Closed, Void
Procore Coverage: Medium — mapped_to_status exists on change events but is not universally applied across all record types
Benchmarking Use: Enables cross-record-type workflow analysis by providing a common status vocabulary
Notes: Procore-specific normalization field. Other systems achieve this through status mapping configurations. Essential for UCDM analytics that span record types.

Category: --- ELECTRONIC SIGNATURE WORKFLOWS: digital signing overlays on standard approval chains ---

Description: A digital signing request sent through an integrated e-signature platform (e.g., DocuSign). Overlays the standard approval workflow — the record's internal status and the e-signature status track independently.
Common Values: Sent, Delivered, Viewed, Signed, Completed, Declined, Voided
Source Systems: Procore (DocuSign integration); PlanGrid; Autodesk Build; Aconex
Procore Coverage: Medium — docusign_status field on commitments, commitment COs, and potential COs. Integration exists but not all records use it.
Benchmarking Use: E-signature adoption rate and signing cycle time benchmark digital transformation maturity
Notes: docusign_status tracks the e-sign lifecycle independently from the record's approval status. Both must be 'complete' for full execution.

Description: The signer currently expected to act on a digital signature request. Mirrors the construction ball-in-court concept within the e-signature workflow — identifies who is holding up execution.
Common Values: Sent (awaiting view), Delivered (viewed, not signed), Completed (signed)
Procore Coverage: Medium — docusign_ball_in_court_status field on commitments and change orders. Tracks per-signer status.
Benchmarking Use: E-signature BIC aging reveals which signers delay contract execution — useful for identifying organizational bottlenecks
Notes: Separate from the main BIC tracking. A record can have ball-in-court with an architect for review while simultaneously having e-signature BIC with an owner for execution.

Category: --- STANDARD MEASURES: KPIs and metrics derived from workflow and status data ---

Description: Mean elapsed time from formal submission to final approval decision. The primary workflow efficiency metric. Formula: AVG(approval_date - submitted_date) in business days. Segment by record type, reviewer, and project phase.
Industry Standard: PMI PMBOK (schedule performance); AIA A201 (response time standards)
Common Values: RFIs: 7-14 days. Submittals: 14-21 days. COs: 14-30 days.
Procore Coverage: High — computable from native date fields on RFIs, submittals, change orders, and most approval records
Benchmarking Use: Core benchmark: compare cycle times across projects, companies, and record types to identify best-in-class performers
Notes: Exclude Draft and Void records. Use business days (exclude weekends). Filter by ball-in-court party for accountability attribution.

Description: Percentage of records that exceed their due date before reaching the expected next state. Formula: COUNT(overdue_records) / COUNT(records_with_due_dates). Segment by record type and responsible party.
Common Values: RFIs: 10-25% overdue typical. Submittals: 15-30% overdue typical.
Procore Coverage: High — overdue flag on submittals, due_date_variance on RFIs, computable on any record with due_date
Benchmarking Use: Overdue rates by party type reveal systemic bottlenecks — architect overdue rate vs. sub overdue rate
Notes: Use native overdue boolean on submittals. For RFIs, use due_date_variance > 0. For other records, compute from due_date vs. status transition date.

Description: Percentage of submissions approved on first review without requiring revision or resubmission. Formula: COUNT(approved_first_pass) / COUNT(total_submissions). Higher rate = better pre-submission quality.
Common Values: Submittals: 60-80% first-pass typical. COs: 85-95%.
Procore Coverage: Medium — requires tracking revision count or submission cycle number. Procore tracks submittal revisions but not universally.
Benchmarking Use: First-pass rates by trade, spec section, and project phase identify where coordination quality is strongest and weakest
Notes: Requires identifying the first submission vs. resubmissions. On submittals, revision number tracks this. On other records, may need to count status transitions.

Description: Percentage of submissions that are rejected or returned for revision before eventual approval. Formula: COUNT(rejected_or_returned) / COUNT(total_submissions). The inverse complement of first-pass approval.
Common Values: Submittals: 20-40% resubmission typical. High-complexity: 30-50%.
Procore Coverage: Medium — computable from status history showing Rejected or Revise and Resubmit transitions
Benchmarking Use: Resubmission rates by spec division reveal which building systems generate the most design coordination churn
Notes: Track not just rate but resubmission count per item. 1 resubmission = normal. 3+ = systemic coordination failure. Cross-reference with CSI division.

Description: Average time a record sits with the current responsible party before action is taken. Formula: AVG(current_date - bic_assignment_date) for active records. Identifies the slowest link in the workflow chain.
Common Values: Architect: 5-10 days avg. GC PM: 2-5 days avg. Owner: 7-14 days avg.
Procore Coverage: High — rfis_bic and submittal_ball_in_courts tables track BIC history with timestamps
Benchmarking Use: BIC aging by party type is the most actionable accountability metric — names the bottleneck
Notes: Compute from BIC assignment timestamp to response/transition timestamp. Current active BIC aging = current_date - last_bic_change.

Description: Average time a record spends in each status before transitioning to the next state. Identifies which statuses are bottlenecks in the workflow. Formula: AVG(next_status_date - current_status_date) per status.
Common Values: Draft: 2-5 days. Pending: 3-7 days. Under Review: 5-14 days.
Procore Coverage: Medium — requires status change history. Available through changelog or *_in_status_since fields on financial records.
Benchmarking Use: Dwell time heat maps by status and record type reveal exactly where workflows stall — actionable process improvement
Notes: Financial records have *_in_status_since columns (e.g., budget_stage_in_status_since). Other records require changelog analysis.

Description: Percentage of records that reach a terminal state (Closed, Approved, Complete) vs. remaining active or stalled. Formula: COUNT(terminal_status) / COUNT(total_created) over a time period.
Common Values: Punch items: 85-95% close rate. RFIs: 90-98%. Submittals: 80-90%.
Procore Coverage: High — computable from status fields on all record types
Benchmarking Use: Completion rates by project phase reveal whether teams close items or let them accumulate — backlog growth signals project health risk
Notes: Filter by creation date cohort to track completion curves over time. Exclude Void from numerator but include in denominator.

Description: Percentage of workflow items that require elevation to a higher authority because the assigned party failed to act within the allowed time. Formula: COUNT(escalated) / COUNT(total_actionable). Higher rate = more workflow friction.
Common Values: 5-15% escalation rate typical on well-run projects. >25% signals systemic problems.
Procore Coverage: None — Procore does not track escalation events natively. Must be inferred from BIC changes after overdue threshold.
Benchmarking Use: Escalation rates benchmark process discipline — low rates indicate responsive teams, high rates indicate capacity or authority problems
Notes: Since Procore lacks native escalation, approximate by identifying BIC reassignments that occur after the due date has passed.
